title: Eton mess
subtitle: Crushed meringue, whipped cream, strawberries.
excerpt: Strawberries macerated with sugar and crushed meringue folded through lightly whipped cream. Three components, no cooking. The meringue can be bought or made ahead. Done in 15 minutes. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Eton mess is traditionally associated with Eton College's cricket match against Harrow; early written references appear in school accounts from the 1890s. The recipe is so simple it requires no formal source; the strawberry-cream-meringue combination appears in British recipe collections from the late 19th century onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The meringues can be made days ahead and stored in an airtight tin. The macerated strawberries can sit for an hour. Assemble at the last minute so the meringue stays crisp.

content.text: The mess is the point. No careful layering, no architectural presentation. Meringue broken up into chunks, cream whipped until it just holds a shape, strawberries cut and left to sit in a little sugar until they release juice. Everything folded together roughly just before serving. It looks dishevelled and it tastes very good.

Shop-bought meringue nests work here. Made from scratch is better: the shells crack and the inside stays chewy. The strawberries should be ripe; pale, flavourless ones won't rescue themselves under sugar. The cream is lightly whipped, not stiff, so the whole thing ripples and billows rather than sitting in thick slabs.

items.prepNote: hulled and halved or quartered if large
for the meringue; or use 4 bought meringue nests (about 200 g)

attrs.heading: Bought or made meringue?
attrs.body: Bought meringue nests save 90 minutes and still give a good result. The difference is texture: shop-bought meringue is uniformly crisp throughout, while a freshly baked one has a crackly shell and a chewy marshmallow centre. Both work. If you go bought, pick up four standard meringue nests (about 50 g each) from any supermarket.

content.text: Toss {{strawberries}} of strawberries with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in a bowl. Leave for 15 to 30 minutes until the berries release some juice. Don't over-macerate; you want them juicy, not pulped.

Pour {{double-cream}} of cold double cream into a clean bowl. Add {{icing-sugar}} of icing sugar and the vanilla extract if using. Whip by hand or with a mixer until the cream just holds soft peaks; it should flow slowly off the whisk, not sit stiffly. Stop short of stiff.

Break the meringues into rough chunks into the cream bowl. Add three-quarters of the strawberries and all their juice. Fold together with a large spoon, six to eight turns only. Streaks of cream, bright red juice and pale meringue pieces should all be visible. Spoon into glasses or bowls and add the reserved strawberries on top.

items.symptom: Meringue turned into a soggy paste
items.cause: Assembled too far in advance, or the strawberry juice was too liquid.
items.fix: Assemble no more than 10 minutes before serving. If using bought meringue, break it into larger pieces; they hold their crunch longer.
Stop as soon as the cream holds soft peaks; a few strokes too many and the fat begins to separate. Add a splash of cold cream and fold gently to loosen it.

items.cause: Strawberries were out of season or under-ripe.
items.fix: Add an extra tablespoon of caster sugar to the maceration and let them sit longer. A few drops of balsamic vinegar can intensify flavour in a pinch.
Leave it messier. The dish should look as though it fell together, not as though it was plated.

content.text: Eton mess is served at Eton College's Fourth of June celebrations, the school's annual speech day and cricket match, and is associated with the height of the English summer. The origin story is probably apocryphal; accounts suggest a meringue dessert was crushed by a dog or dropped in transit, and the name stuck. It belongs at picnics, garden parties and anywhere strawberry season coincides with the need for something that takes no time at all.
